title: RAM Sizing Guide
summary: Understand workload type, process or VM concurrency, per-process memory demand, operating-system overhead, cache reserve, and practical memory headroom.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether the memory allocation can support the workload without excessive paging, cache starvation, or unstable operating margin.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when sizing servers, virtual machines, database hosts, application workloads, analytics systems, or multi-service compute nodes.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible RAM Sizing Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects memory pressure.

items.label: Workload Type
items.description: The broad workload profile that influences memory behavior and reserve expectations.
items.whyItMatters: Workload type changes how much memory should be reserved for cache, buffers, and active service behavior.
items.commonMistake: Using the same RAM estimate for every workload just because the CPU requirement looked similar.

items.label: Concurrent Processes / VMs
items.description: The number of processes, VMs, services, jobs, or active workload instances expected to run at the same time.
items.whyItMatters: Concurrency multiplies per-process memory demand and can turn a small application footprint into a large host requirement.
items.commonMistake: Sizing memory for a single process or VM and forgetting the full active count.

items.label: Avg RAM per Process (GB)
items.description: The average memory consumed by each process, VM, workload instance, or service unit.
items.whyItMatters: This is the core per-unit memory value that gets multiplied by concurrency.
items.commonMistake: Using idle memory instead of realistic loaded memory.

items.label: OS / Base Overhead (GB)
items.description: Memory reserved for the operating system, hypervisor, management services, agents, file cache, or baseline platform overhead.
items.whyItMatters: The platform needs memory before the application workload receives anything.
items.commonMistake: Forgetting OS, hypervisor, antivirus, monitoring, and background service overhead.

items.label: Cache / Headroom (%)
items.description: Extra memory reserved above calculated demand for cache, buffers, spikes, leaks, and growth.
examples: More reserve for databases or virtualization
items.whyItMatters: Headroom keeps the workload from running at the edge and can reduce disk pressure caused by paging or cache misses.
items.commonMistake: Treating unused RAM as waste instead of stability margin.

links.label: See storage pressure from memory limits

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means memory has practical reserve. Watch means the allocation may work but deserves review. Risk means the workload may page, stall, reduce cache efficiency, or become unreliable under load.
bullets: Recommended RAM is rounded for practical planning.
Reserve ratio matters because small spare capacity disappears quickly during bursts.
If memory is tight, storage I/O may become worse because paging and cache misses increase.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: RAM Sizing follows CPU Sizing because memory pressure can invalidate an otherwise reasonable CPU plan.
bullets: Keep workload and concurrency assumptions aligned with CPU Sizing.
Use the resulting memory profile before estimating storage IOPS.
Avoid moving deeper into the flow if CPU and RAM assumptions conflict.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
